title: AgentCore Managed Harness: Getting Started
summary: Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Managed Harness is a service that simplifies AI agent deployment by replacing manual container and orchestration setup with JSON config and system prompts. It provides isolated micro VMs, persistent storage, cross-session memory, and hot-swappable models and tools, with integrations to browsers, code interpreters, and external services like Telegram.
writeup: Amazon Bedrock AgentCore's managed harness collapses the production infrastructure problem, compute, containers, storage, memory, auth, and observability, into a JSON config file and a system prompt. The video builds a working agent called Pulse that scrapes Hacker News via a managed browser and Reddit via RSS on an hourly EventBridge schedule, accumulates JSON snapshots in persistent storage at /mount/data, and delivers a six-hour digest to Telegram. The through-line is that the operational boilerplate that makes running agent loops in production hard has been absorbed by the platform, leaving the developer to define behavior rather than infrastructure.

similar.title: How to Set Up and Use SpaceBot (Better Than OpenClaw?)

key_points: The AgentCore managed harness provisions an isolated microVM per session with its own shell, file system, and cross-session memory scoped by user; persistent storage at /mount/data survives VM restarts and accumulates data across hourly invocations because the same session ID is reused.
Running shell commands on the VM with the --exec flag costs no additional tokens, making file-system operations and lightweight orchestration essentially free relative to LLM calls.

Deploying an agent is three steps: define via config (harness.json plus a system prompt Markdown file), run agentcore deploy (which provisions CDK-backed IAM roles and memory, taking three to five minutes), then invoke via agentcore invoke; model, tools, and system prompt can all be swapped per API call without redeploying.
The actor ID parameter on each invocation scopes the memory namespace, so the scheduled Lambda and future per-user chat sessions each maintain separate memory without collision.

The Lambda that drives the schedule uses a single invoke-harness API call passing the harness ARN, a session ID, an actor ID, and a prompt; the prompt content switches between collection mode and summary mode based on whether the UTC hour is divisible by six.

Telegram delivery is handled in the Lambda after the harness responds: a chunking function splits the digest to respect Telegram's 4,096-character message limit, and the bot token is fetched from Secrets Manager rather than an environment variable.
